{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-12","facts":["Intrinsic motivation comes from interest or enjoyment in the activity itself.","Extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards, pressure, or consequences.","The overjustification effect occurs when external rewards reduce intrinsic motivation.","Informational rewards can support motivation when they signal competence.","Controlling rewards can undermine motivation when they feel coercive."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"intrinsic motivation","df":"A drive to engage in an activity because it is enjoyable, interesting, or personally satisfying.","eg":"Reading psychology articles because you find the topic fascinating.","mi":"It means a person is always more productive without any outside incentives.","cx":"Intrinsic motivation refers to the source of motivation, not guaranteed productivity or effort."},{"tr":"extrinsic motivation","df":"A drive to act because of external outcomes such as praise, grades, money, or avoiding punishment.","eg":"Studying to earn a high exam score.","mi":"It only matters when a person does not care about the task at all.","cx":"Extrinsic motivation can matter even when the person also values the activity itself."},{"tr":"overjustification effect","df":"A decline in intrinsic interest after external rewards are introduced for an already enjoyable activity.","eg":"A child who liked drawing less after being paid for each picture.","mi":"Any reward always destroys interest in every task.","cx":"The effect is most likely when rewards are expected and controlling for an already interesting activity."}],"scenario":{"su":"A student enjoys sketching in free time. A school starts paying students each week for every sketch they submit, and the student initially draws more often. After several weeks, the student stops sketching when payment ends and reports that drawing now feels like work.","ma":"The student’s behavior before, during, and after payment; the change in reported enjoyment."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Should schools use rewards to encourage behaviors students already enjoy, such as reading or art?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because rewards can increase short-term participation but also risk reducing intrinsic interest.","po":["Use rewards sparingly because they may undermine enjoyment of already interesting activities.","Use rewards when participation matters more than preserving intrinsic interest.","Use rewards only when they are informational rather than controlling."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze a real or hypothetical activity that people often find enjoyable, then predict whether a reward would likely increase participation, preserve interest, or produce overjustification. Explain the prediction using intrinsic motivation, extrinsic motivation, and reward type.","pa":["250-400 words","must distinguish informational versus controlling rewards","must include one concrete example of likely overjustification","must avoid assuming all rewards have the same effect"]},"mc":[{"q":"Which statement best defines intrinsic motivation?","op":["Motivation that comes from interest, enjoyment, or satisfaction in the activity itself.","Motivation that comes from grades, money, praise, or punishment.","Motivation that disappears whenever a task becomes difficult.","Motivation that is present only when no one is watching."],"ai":0,"ex":"Intrinsic motivation is driven by the activity itself, not by external consequences.","fi":[0]},{"q":"A child loves painting. After being paid for each finished painting, the child paints less when the payment stops. Which effect is this?","op":["The overjustification effect","Classical conditioning","Social facilitation","Sensory adaptation"],"ai":0,"ex":"External rewards reduced the child’s later interest in an activity that was already enjoyable.","fi":[2]},{"q":"A teacher says, 'You can choose any topic for your project, and this option shows your creativity.' Why is this reward more likely to support motivation than a controlling reward?","op":["It feels informational and supports competence rather than pressure.","It removes all external incentives from the task.","It guarantees the student will never lose interest.","It makes the task harder for everyone."],"ai":0,"ex":"Information that affirms competence can support intrinsic motivation better than pressure-based control.","fi":[3]},{"q":"Which claim about rewards and motivation is most accurate?","op":["Rewards always reduce intrinsic motivation for every task.","Rewards can increase behavior but sometimes lower interest in already enjoyable tasks.","Extrinsic motivation is identical to intrinsic motivation in practice.","Overjustification only occurs with punishment, not rewards."],"ai":1,"ex":"Rewards can be effective for behavior, but they may undermine interest when the activity was already intrinsically rewarding.","fi":[3,4]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"Connect intrinsic motivation, extrinsic motivation, and overjustification effect by explaining these claims: Intrinsic motivation comes from interest or enjoyment in the activity itself. Extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards, pressure, or consequences.","rs":"Retrieve each definition and correction, then apply the three terms to the scenario without notes."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-6","facts":["Light is an electromagnetic wave characterized by wavelength and frequency.","Across the electromagnetic spectrum, shorter wavelengths correspond to higher frequencies and higher energies.","Visible light is only a narrow band within the larger electromagnetic spectrum.","Astronomers infer temperature from the peak wavelength of an object's emitted light.","Dust and gas can absorb or scatter some wavelengths more effectively than others."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"electromagnetic spectrum","df":"The full range of electromagnetic radiation, ordered by wavelength or frequency from long-wavelength radio waves to short-wavelength gamma rays.","eg":"Radio waves, visible light, and X-rays are all parts of the electromagnetic spectrum.","mi":"The spectrum is only the colors humans can see.","cx":"Visible light is just one small segment of a much broader range of radiation."},{"tr":"wavelength","df":"The distance between repeating points on a wave, such as crest to crest, usually measured in meters or related units.","eg":"Visible red light has a longer wavelength than blue light.","mi":"A longer wavelength means the light is always brighter.","cx":"Wavelength describes wave size, not brightness; brightness depends on intensity and source conditions."},{"tr":"frequency","df":"The number of wave cycles passing a point each second, measured in hertz.","eg":"Blue light has a higher frequency than red light.","mi":"Frequency and wavelength increase together for light.","cx":"For electromagnetic radiation, frequency increases as wavelength decreases."}],"scenario":{"su":"An astronomer observes two objects: one appears bright in infrared but faint in visible light, while another is strong in ultraviolet and weak in infrared. The same region also contains a thick dust cloud that blocks some starlight.","ma":"A set of observations in radio, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, and X-ray bands for the two objects and the dust cloud."},"discussionPrompt":{"pr":"Should astronomers rely on visible light as their primary window for studying the universe, or do multiwavelength observations provide a more complete picture?","tn":"Reasonable positions differ because visible light is easiest to detect, but other wavelengths reveal different physics and penetrate dust differently.","po":["Visible light is a practical starting point because it is familiar and often well-resolved.","Multiwavelength data are essential because different wavelengths reveal different temperatures and processes.","The best approach depends on the target, since dust, temperature, and emission mechanisms vary."]},"assignmentCore":{"td":"Analyze the scenario by identifying what the infrared-bright object, ultraviolet-bright object, and dust cloud suggest about temperature and wavelength-dependent visibility. Explain which wavelength bands would be most useful for each component and why.","pa":["Use 1-2 paragraphs.","Reference at least three wavelength regions.","Do not use numerical calculations.","Support claims with wavelength, temperature, or dust-penetration reasoning."]},"mc":[{"q":"Which statement best describes the electromagnetic spectrum?","op":["It is the full range of electromagnetic radiation ordered by wavelength or frequency.","It is the set of only the colors visible to the human eye.","It is the range of sound waves produced by astronomical objects.","It is the sequence of planets arranged by distance from the Sun."],"ai":0,"ex":"The electromagnetic spectrum includes all electromagnetic radiation, not just visible light.","fi":[0,2]},{"q":"A cloud of dust blocks visible starlight but allows more infrared light to pass. What is the best explanation?","op":["Infrared light has a longer wavelength and is less easily blocked by dust.","Infrared light has a shorter wavelength and is more easily scattered by dust.","Infrared light is not electromagnetic radiation, so dust does not affect it.","Infrared light always comes only from cooler objects, so dust absorbs it less."],"ai":0,"ex":"Longer-wavelength infrared light often penetrates dust better than visible light.","fi":[0,4]},{"q":"Two stars have different colors: Star A is redder and Star B is bluer. What can be inferred?","op":["Star B is hotter and emits more short-wavelength light than Star A.","Star A is hotter and emits more short-wavelength light than Star B.","Star A and Star B must have the same temperature because color does not matter.","Star B must be closer than Star A because blue light travels faster."],"ai":0,"ex":"Bluer objects generally peak at shorter wavelengths and indicate higher temperatures.","fi":[1,4]},{"q":"A claim says that visible light gives a complete picture of all astronomical objects because all objects emit visible radiation. How should this claim be evaluated?","op":["It is incomplete because many objects emit strongly at non-visible wavelengths and dust can obscure visible light.","It is correct because all astronomical objects emit equally in visible light.","It is correct because visible light has the highest energy in the spectrum.","It is incomplete only because radio waves cannot be detected from space."],"ai":0,"ex":"Different objects emit at different wavelengths, and many are better studied outside the visible range.","fi":[1,4]}],"studyGuide":{"sm":"['Electromagnetic radiation is organized by wavelength and frequency.', 'Shorter wavelength means higher frequency and higher energy.', 'Visible light is only a small part of the spectrum.', 'Temperature affects the peak wavelength of emitted radiation.', 'Different wavelengths interact differently with dust and gas.']","rs":"['Compare adjacent bands to remember the order of the spectrum.', 'Link long wavelength with radio/microwave/infrared and short wavelength with ultraviolet/X-ray/gamma rays.', 'Use the mnemonic that hotter objects peak at shorter wavelengths.']"}}]}

{"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-1","facts":["Classical conditioning pairs two stimuli so one begins to predict the other.","The unconditioned stimulus naturally elicits an unconditioned response.","A conditioned stimulus acquires the ability to trigger a conditioned response.","Acquisition is the initial learning phase when the association is formed.","Extinction occurs when the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"unconditioned stimulus","df":"A stimulus that naturally and automatically triggers a response without prior learning.","eg":"Food that causes salivation in a dog.","mi":"It is any stimulus that becomes important after practice.","cx":"Its effect occurs before and without conditioning; it does not depend on prior association."},{"tr":"conditioned stimulus","df":"A previously neutral stimulus that gains the ability to elicit a response after being paired with an unconditioned stimulus.","eg":"A tone that later makes a dog salivate after being paired with food.","mi":"It is always an innately powerful stimulus.","cx":"It starts as neutral and becomes effective through learning."},{"tr":"extinction","df":"The weakening of a conditioned response when the conditioned stimulus is no longer paired with the unconditioned stimulus.","eg":"A tone is played many times without food until salivation fades.","mi":"It erases the learning completely and permanently.","cx":"The response weakens, but the association can still reappear later."}],"scenario":{"su":"A student hears a phone notification tone every time a message from a close friend arrives. {"lessons":[{"lessonId":"lesson-3","facts":["Comparative advantage is based on lower opportunity cost, not on absolute productivity.","A person or country can have absolute advantage in both goods and still specialize by comparative advantage.","Trade can increase total output when each party specializes in the good with lower opportunity cost.","Opportunity cost measures the value of the next best alternative forgone.","Gains from trade arise when both parties can consume beyond their no-trade production possibilities."],"keyTerms":[{"tr":"comparative advantage","df":"The ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another producer.
